title: Understanding the Problem
text: To find the final velocity of the ozone molecule, we need to use the principle of conservation of momentum. This principle states that the total momentum before the collision is equal to the total momentum after the collision.

title: Calculate Initial Momentum
text: Calculate the initial momentum of the oxygen atom and the oxygen molecule separately. Use the formula: \( $$p=mv$$ \), where \( $$m$$ \) is mass and \( v \) is velocity.

title: Components of Velocity
text: Break down the velocities of the oxygen atom and molecule into their \( \hat{i} \) and \( \hat{j} \) components using trigonometry. For example, \( v_x=v\cos(\theta) \) and \( v_y=v\sin(\theta) \).

title: Calculate \( \hat{i} \) Component of Initial Momentum
text: Calculate the \( \hat{i} \) component of the initial momentum for both the oxygen atom and molecule. Add them together to get the total initial \( \hat{i} \) momentum.

title: Calculate \( \hat{j} \) Component of Initial Momentum
text: Calculate the \( \hat{j} \) component of the initial momentum for both the oxygen atom and molecule. Add them together to get the total initial \( \hat{j} \) momentum.

title: Final Momentum and Velocity
text: The final momentum of the system is the sum of the initial momenta. Since the masses are combined, use the total mass to find the final velocity: \( v_f=\frac{p_f}{m_f} \).

title: Calculate Final Velocity
text: Using the total initial momentum and the combined mass of the ozone molecule, calculate the final velocity components.

title: Verify Your Answer
text: Ensure that your final velocity components match the given answer: \( 19.3\hat{i}+340.6\hat{j} \) $$\frac{m}{s}$$.
